title: Reflections on Bikeshedding in Open Source
summary: Poul-Henning Kamp&\#x27;s article &\#x27;Goodbye, and Thanks for All the Bikesheds&\#x27; offers a retrospective on the bikeshedding phenomenon in open source, providing insights on how to manage trivial decisions effectively. This article matters because bikeshedding wastes community time and energy; understanding it helps open source projects focus on important issues and maintain productivity. The author, PHK, is a prominent FreeBSD developer who created MD5crypt; the article combines historical context with practical advice for avoiding trivial disputes.
context: Bikeshedding, also known as Parkinson&\#x27;s law of triviality, describes the tendency to spend disproportionate time on trivial matters while neglecting important ones. It is a common phenomenon in organizations and open source communities, where simple but unimportant issues attract excessive discussion.
discussion: Commenters highlighted the concept of reversible decisions as a solution to bikeshedding, and noted PHK&\#x27;s contributions like MD5crypt. Some diverged into age restrictions and gender issues in tech.
